title: TF-IDF: Weighting Words
concept: TF-IDF weights words based on their frequency in the document (TF) divided by their frequency across the whole dataset (IDF), highlighting rare, descriptive terms. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Classical NLP.'

exercises.explain: TF-IDF stands for Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency. It helps us weigh how important a word is.

cards.title: Too Many 'The's!
cards.body: Some words are everywhere, like 'is', 'and', or 'it'. If a computer just counts words, it might think these are the most important terms in a document! How do we teach AI to ignore the boring background noise and find the words that actually matter?

cards.title: What is TF-IDF?
cards.body: TF-IDF stands for Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency. It is a smart math tool that scores words. Words that appear a lot in one document (TF) but are rare across all other documents (IDF) get a high score because they are the most descriptive!

cards.title: The Library Search
cards.body: Imagine a library where every book mentions the word 'the' thousands of times. If you search for 'the', you get every book! But if you search for 'dinosaur', you only get books actually about dinosaurs. TF-IDF acts like a librarian who ignores common words ('the') and highlights unique words ('dinosaur') because they carry the real meaning.

cards.title: Recipe Keywords
cards.body: If you analyze a recipe book, the word 'mix' might show up in every recipe. Because it is everywhere, its TF-IDF score drops. But if only one recipe mentions 'cinnamon', that word gets a high TF-IDF score, telling us that 'cinnamon' is a key ingredient for that specific dish!

cards.title: Frequent Isn't Always Important
cards.body: You might think that the most frequent word in a document is always the most important. That's a myth! If 'the' is the most frequent word, it doesn't tell us anything about the topic. TF-IDF proves that rare, specific words are often the most valuable.

cards.title: TF-IDF Weighting
cards.body: Let's look at how TF-IDF scores words differently based on how common they are across your dataset.

left.text: Appears in every document. Result: Low TF-IDF score (ignored as background noise).

right.text: Appears in only one document. Result: High TF-IDF score (marked as highly important).

exercises.prompt: What kind of TF-IDF score would a super common word like 'and' get?

options.text: It doubles every time it appears

exercises.explain: Common words like 'and' appear in almost every document, so TF-IDF gives them a very low score. Rare words get a high score.

exercises.prompt: True or False: TF-IDF gives the highest scores to words that appear in every single document in a dataset.
exercises.explain: TF-IDF gives higher weight to rare words that carry unique meaning, not common filler words.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing part of the TF-IDF acronym!
exercises.sentence: TF stands for Term Frequency, and [blank] stands for Inverse Document Frequency.
exercises.explain: IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) measures how unique or rare a word is across all documents in your collection.

exercises.prompt: Look at this sentence: 'The astronauts boarded the spacecraft and prepared for launch.' Which word gets the highest TF-IDF score?

options.text: 'the' gets the highest score because it appears first.
'spacecraft' gets a high score because it is rare and specific.
'and' gets a high score because it connects words together.

exercises.explain: Sentence B is correct because the word 'spacecraft' is rare and highly descriptive of the topic, earning it a high TF-IDF score. 'The' and 'and' are too common to get high scores.

exercises.prompt: Now for the final challenge! How does TF-IDF help computers understand what a specific document is really about?

options.text: It measures how fast a computer can read a text document.
It counts only the shortest words in a document to save memory space.
It scores words higher if they appear often in one document but rarely in others.
It completely deletes all nouns from a text before analysis.

exercises.explain: TF-IDF helps machines ignore common filler words and focus on unique, meaningful terms. Next up: Classical NLP.
